title: Core Features
description: A deep dive into the nine core features that power a modern Next.js web application, from file-based routing and Redux state management to theming, internationalization, and component architecture patterns.

blocks.heading: Core Features
blocks.body: This section explores the nine major features used in a production Next.js application. Each feature is explained from first principles with real code examples drawn from a working codebase.

In a traditional website, every page is a separate HTML file. If you want an "About" page, you create about.html. Next.js works the same way, except instead of HTML files you create folders and TypeScript files. This is called file-based routing -- the folder structure is the URL structure.
Every folder inside src/app/ becomes a URL path. Every file named page.tsx inside that folder becomes the page the user sees. No configuration files, no route tables -- just folders and files.

blocks.heading: How folders map to URLs
blocks.body: The folder structure directly determines your site's URL paths.

blocks.code:
src/app/
  page.tsx                    --> yoursite.com/
  getting-started/
    page.tsx                  --> yoursite.com/getting-started
  fundamentals/
    page.tsx                  --> yoursite.com/fundamentals
  features/
    page.tsx                  --> yoursite.com/features
  exam/
    page.tsx                  --> yoursite.com/exam

blocks.body: That is it. Create a folder, put a page.tsx in it, and you have a new page on your website. No imports, no registration -- Next.js finds it automatically.

blocks.heading: Dynamic routes with [slug]
blocks.body: Sometimes you need a page that works for many different values. For example, a blog might have /blog/my-first-post and /blog/react-tips. Instead of creating a folder for every post, you create a dynamic route by wrapping the folder name in square brackets.

blocks.code:
src/app/
  blog/
    [slug]/
      page.tsx                --> /blog/my-first-post, /blog/react-tips, etc.

blocks.description: Reading the dynamic slug parameter from the URL

blocks.heading: Layouts wrap pages automatically
blocks.body: A layout.tsx file wraps every page in its folder (and all sub-folders) with shared UI. The locale layout adds the Header, Footer, theme provider, and Redux store around every page -- so each page only contains its own unique content.

blocks.description: A layout that wraps every page with shared providers and UI

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Routing in Next.js is just folders and files. Create a folder, add a page.tsx, and that folder name becomes the URL. Use [brackets] for dynamic segments and layout.tsx to share UI across pages.

React components can hold their own data using useState, but some data needs to be shared across many components. For example, whether the user prefers dark mode needs to be known by the theme provider, the toggle button, and possibly individual components. Passing that data through dozens of components manually (called prop drilling) gets messy fast.

Redux solves this by creating a single store that holds shared state. Any component anywhere in the app can read from the store or update it. Redux Toolkit is the modern, simplified version of Redux that removes most of the boilerplate.

blocks.heading: Creating the store with makeStore()
blocks.body: The store combines all feature reducers into one central state object.

blocks.description: Configuring the Redux store with multiple feature slices

blocks.heading: A slice is a piece of the store
blocks.body: Each feature gets its own "slice" of state. A slice defines the initial data, plus the actions (functions) that can change it. Here is the theme slice that controls dark and light mode:

blocks.description: A Redux slice for managing dark/light theme mode

blocks.heading: Typed hooks for reading and updating state
blocks.body: Instead of using the generic React-Redux hooks, you create typed versions. This gives you autocomplete and catches type errors at build time.

blocks.description: Typed Redux hooks for use throughout the application

blocks.heading: StoreProvider wraps the app
blocks.body: The store needs to be available to every component. A provider component wraps the app and makes the store accessible through React context.

blocks.description: Provider component that makes the Redux store available to all children

blocks.heading: Using state in a component
blocks.body: Components read from the store with useAppSelector and dispatch actions with useAppDispatch.

blocks.description: A component that reads and toggles the theme from Redux state

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Redux Toolkit gives your app a single store for shared state. Each feature gets a slice with its data and actions. Components read state with useAppSelector and update it with useAppDispatch. The StoreProvider in the layout makes it all work.

Material UI (MUI) has a built-in theming system. You define your colors, typography, and component styles in one place, and every MUI component in your app automatically uses them. Switching between dark and light mode means swapping the entire color palette at once.

blocks.heading: The getTheme() function
blocks.body: This function takes a mode ("light" or "dark") and returns a complete MUI theme object. It defines the primary color, background colors, text colors, border radius, and overrides for specific components.

blocks.description: Theme factory function that produces light or dark MUI themes

blocks.heading: ThemeRegistry with Emotion cache
blocks.body: The ThemeRegistry component reads the current theme mode from Redux, builds the theme, and wraps the app with MUI's ThemeProvider. The Emotion cache with prepend: true ensures that MUI styles load before your custom styles, so your overrides always win.

blocks.description: Bridges Redux state to MUI theming with Emotion cache

blocks.heading: How the toggle connects to the theme
blocks.body: The flow works like this: the user clicks the toggle button, which dispatches toggleTheme() to Redux. Redux updates the mode in the store. ThemeRegistry reads the new mode and rebuilds the theme. MUI re-renders every component with the new colors. The entire switch happens in a single render cycle.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Define your colors and component overrides in a single getTheme() function. ThemeRegistry bridges Redux state to MUI, so toggling dark mode is just a Redux action. CssBaseline resets browser defaults so your theme is applied consistently.

Internationalization (i18n for short -- there are 18 letters between the "i" and "n") means building your app so it can support multiple languages. Localization (l10n) means actually translating it into a specific language. Think of i18n as building the plumbing, and l10n as connecting the water.

The application uses next-intl, a library built specifically for Next.js. Instead of hardcoding English text in your components, you put translation keys and look up the actual text from JSON files. Switching languages means swapping which JSON file gets loaded.

blocks.heading: Define your supported locales
blocks.body: The routing configuration specifies which languages are supported and which is the default.

blocks.description: Locale routing configuration for next-intl

blocks.heading: The [locale] folder handles language routing
blocks.body: By placing all your pages inside a [locale] folder, Next.js captures the language code from the URL. Visiting /en/features sets the locale to English. Visiting /es/features would set it to Spanish.

blocks.code:
src/app/
  [locale]/                   <-- captures language from URL
    layout.tsx                <-- loads the right translations
    page.tsx                  <-- home page
    getting-started/
      page.tsx
    features/
      page.tsx

blocks.heading: Translation messages in JSON
blocks.body: Each language gets its own JSON file. The keys stay the same across all languages, but the values are translated.

blocks.description: English translation messages for navigation labels

blocks.heading: The useTranslations hook
blocks.body: In your components, you never write raw English strings for UI labels. Instead, you call useTranslations with a namespace (like "nav") and use the t() function to look up each key.

blocks.description: Using the useTranslations hook to render localized navigation labels

blocks.heading: Middleware routes users to the right locale
blocks.body: The middleware intercepts every request and checks the URL for a locale prefix.

blocks.description: Next.js middleware that routes users to the correct locale

blocks.body: The middleware intercepts every request and checks the URL for a locale. If none is found, it redirects to the default locale. This happens on the server before any page renders.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: i18n separates your text from your components. Put translated strings in JSON files, use useTranslations to look them up, and the [locale] folder structure plus middleware handle the rest. Adding a new language means creating a new JSON file.

By default, all Redux state lives in memory. When the user refreshes the page or closes the browser tab, the state vanishes. The theme goes back to light mode, exam progress resets, and bookmarks disappear. That is a terrible user experience.

Redux Persist solves this by automatically saving parts of your Redux store to localStorage (the browser's built-in key-value storage). When the page loads again, it reads the saved data and restores the store to its previous state. The user never notices anything happened.

blocks.heading: The persist configuration
blocks.body: The whitelist array controls which slices get saved. Not everything should be persisted -- temporary UI state or data that should be fetched fresh does not belong in localStorage.

blocks.description: Redux Persist configuration with whitelist for selective persistence

blocks.heading: PersistGate delays rendering until state is loaded
blocks.body: When the page loads, Redux Persist needs a moment to read from localStorage and restore the state. PersistGate shows nothing (or a loading spinner) until the state is ready. Without it, you would briefly see light mode flash before dark mode kicks in.

blocks.description: StoreProvider with PersistGate to prevent flash-of-default-state

blocks.heading: What to persist vs what not to
blocks.body: Choose carefully which state to save to localStorage.

blocks.heading: Persistence decision guide
rows: Data that should be fetched fresh from an API
Form drafts the user might want back
Large objects that would slow down localStorage

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Redux Persist saves your store to localStorage automatically. Use a whitelist to control which slices are saved. PersistGate prevents flash-of-default-state on page load. Only persist data the user would expect to survive a refresh.

Responsive design means your website looks good on any screen size -- from a phone to a wide desktop monitor. Material UI makes this straightforward with its Grid2 layout system and the sx prop breakpoints.

blocks.heading: MUI Grid2 for layouts
blocks.body: Grid2 divides the screen into 12 columns. You tell each item how many columns it should take up at different screen sizes. On small screens a card might take all 12 columns (full width), but on medium screens it takes 6 (half width), and on large screens it takes 4 (one third).

blocks.description: Using MUI Grid2 for responsive column layouts

blocks.heading: The sx prop with breakpoints
blocks.body: The sx prop accepts objects where keys are breakpoint names. This lets you set different values for different screen sizes right in your JSX -- no separate CSS files, no media queries to write by hand.

blocks.description: Using sx prop breakpoints for responsive padding, font sizes, and visibility

blocks.heading: The mobile drawer pattern
blocks.body: On desktop, the header shows navigation links in a horizontal bar. On mobile, there is not enough room, so the links are hidden and a hamburger menu icon appears instead. Tapping it opens a Drawer (a panel that slides in from the side) with all the navigation links stacked vertically.

blocks.description: Responsive navigation with desktop links and mobile drawer

blocks.heading: Container maxWidth
blocks.body: The Container component centers your content and limits its maximum width so it does not stretch across ultra-wide monitors. Common values are "sm" (600px), "md" (900px), and "lg" (1200px).

blocks.description: The standard page container pattern

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Use Grid2 size to control column spans at each breakpoint. Use sx with breakpoint objects for responsive padding, font sizes, and visibility. The Container component prevents content from stretching too wide. Desktop nav links + mobile drawer is the standard responsive navigation pattern.

A practical rule of thumb: if your component's TSX file is longer than about 50 lines, it is probably doing too much. Custom hooks let you extract logic (state, event handlers, computed values) out of the component and into a separate file, so the component file stays short and focused on what it looks like.

blocks.heading: The useHeader hook
blocks.body: The Header component needs to manage several pieces of state: whether the mobile drawer is open, and whether the "More" menu is open. Instead of putting all that logic in the component, it lives in a custom hook.

blocks.description: Custom hook that extracts all Header state and handlers

blocks.heading: The component stays clean
blocks.body: Now the Header component just calls the hook and uses the returned values. All the state management logic is out of sight.

blocks.description: Clean Header component that delegates logic to useHeader hook

blocks.heading: Naming conventions and hook composition
blocks.body: Follow these conventions when creating custom hooks.

items: Hook files always start with "use" -- this is a React requirement, not just a convention. React uses the "use" prefix to enforce the rules of hooks.
Name the hook after the component it serves: useHeader for Header, useCountdown for a countdown timer.
Hooks can call other hooks. A useQuiz hook might use useCountdown internally. This is called hook composition.
Keep hooks in a src/hooks/ folder so they are easy to find.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Custom hooks extract logic from components so your TSX files stay short and readable. Name them with the use prefix, keep them in src/hooks/, and return an object with the state and handlers the component needs. Hooks can use other hooks for composition.

A well-organized codebase is one where you can find any file in seconds. The application uses a feature-based folder structure where related components live together, and each component is small enough to understand at a glance.

blocks.heading: Feature-based folder organization
blocks.body: Components are grouped by the feature they belong to, not by their type.

blocks.description: Feature-based component folder structure
blocks.code:
components/
  layout/                 <-- Shared structural components
    Header.tsx            <-- Top navigation bar
    Footer.tsx            <-- Bottom of every page
    MobileDrawer.tsx      <-- Mobile slide-out menu
    ThemeToggle.tsx       <-- Dark/light mode switch

  common/                 <-- Reusable across all features
    CodeBlock.tsx         <-- Syntax-highlighted code display
    BookmarkButton.tsx    <-- Bookmark toggle button
    LoadingSpinner.tsx    <-- Loading indicator
    GlossaryTooltip.tsx   <-- Hover definition tooltips

  exam/                   <-- Everything for the exam feature
    QuestionCard.tsx      <-- Displays one question
    AnswerOption.tsx      <-- Single answer choice
    ScoreCircle.tsx       <-- Circular progress indicator
    ExamTimer.tsx         <-- Countdown timer
    ExamProgress.tsx      <-- Progress bar

  home/                   <-- Homepage-specific components
    HeroSection.tsx       <-- Big banner at the top
    FeatureCard.tsx       <-- Feature highlight card
    CtaSection.tsx        <-- Call-to-action section

blocks.heading: The 50-line rule
blocks.body: If a component grows beyond about 50 lines, it is a sign that it has too many responsibilities. The solution is to extract parts of it into smaller sub-components. Each sub-component does one thing well.

blocks.description: A single component doing too many things
The same page broken into focused sub-components

blocks.heading: When to use props, context, or Redux
blocks.body: Choose the right data-passing mechanism based on your needs.

blocks.heading: Data passing strategies
rows: Pass data directly from parent to child. Best when data only goes one or two levels deep. Example: passing a question object to QuestionCard.
For data that many components need but that does not change often. Example: the current locale, or a form's validation state across many fields.
For state shared across unrelated parts of the app that changes frequently. Example: theme mode, user bookmarks, exam progress that must survive page navigation.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Organize by feature, not by type. Keep components under 50 lines by extracting sub-components. Use props for parent-child data, context for wide but stable data, and Redux for global state that changes often and needs to persist.

A common beginner mistake is embedding all your data (questions, navigation links, page content) directly in your components. This makes files huge and mixes up what to show with how to show it. The proper pattern separates these concerns cleanly.

blocks.heading: JSON files for data (the what)
blocks.body: Data lives in JSON files. This keeps your data portable, easy to edit, and completely separate from your presentation logic. Need to add a new exam question? Edit the JSON file. Need to change how questions look? Edit the component. They never interfere with each other.

blocks.description: Exam question data stored as pure JSON

blocks.heading: TSX components for presentation (the how)
blocks.body: The component only knows HOW to display a question. It does not know or care where the data came from.

blocks.description: A presentation component that displays any question data it receives

blocks.heading: Linking multiple JSON files to keep file size down
blocks.body: If you have hundreds of exam questions, putting them all in one file makes it slow to load and hard to navigate. Instead, split data into multiple files and import only what you need.

blocks.description: Central index file that links to all data files
Importing only the data needed for a specific page

blocks.heading: Why this separation matters
blocks.body: Separating data from presentation provides three key benefits.

blocks.heading: Benefits of data/styling separation
rows: Non-developers can edit JSON files without understanding React. A subject-matter expert can add questions without touching any TSX code.
The same QuestionCard component can display questions from any chapter, any topic, or even a completely different quiz -- just feed it different data.
When data is split into separate files, the browser only downloads what is needed for the current page instead of loading everything upfront.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Keep data in JSON files, presentation in TSX components. Split large data sets across multiple files and import only what each page needs. This separation makes your app easier to maintain, your components reusable, and your bundle sizes smaller.

keyPoints: File-based routing in Next.js maps folders to URLs -- create a folder with a page.tsx and you have a new route
Redux Toolkit provides a centralized store with typed slices for shared state management across components
MUI theming with a getTheme() factory function enables seamless dark/light mode switching via Redux
Internationalization with next-intl separates translatable text into JSON files looked up via useTranslations
Redux Persist saves selected state slices to localStorage so user preferences survive page refreshes
Responsive design uses MUI Grid2 column spans and sx breakpoint objects instead of manual CSS media queries
Custom hooks extract component logic into reusable files, keeping TSX components focused on presentation
Feature-based folder organization groups related components together and enforces the 50-line component rule
Separating data (JSON) from presentation (TSX) makes content easy to update and components reusable
